2 Simulate Data

3 Oracle Forgiveness Curves

4 DAG: Time-Dependent Confounding

  [saved: fig-dag]

4.1 Why time-varying confounding breaks standard regression

The following sequence of DAGs illustrates step by step why standard approaches fail when L is both a confounder and a mediator. Each figure highlights specific causal paths.

4.1.1 Step 1: The full causal structure (all paths)

All arrows shown equally — this is the data-generating process. PDC (adherence) at each block is affected by health (L), and adherence in turn affects future health — the feedback loop.

  [saved: fig-dag-step1]

4.1.2 Step 2: The causal effect of interest

We want to estimate the effect of adherence (PDC) on outcome (Y). The direct causal paths from PDC to Y are highlighted.

  [saved: fig-dag-step2]

4.1.3 Step 3: The confounding problem — L confounds PDC-Y

Health (L) is a common cause of both adherence (PDC) and outcome (Y). Without adjustment, the PDC-Y association is confounded: sicker patients have worse adherence AND worse outcomes.

  [saved: fig-dag-step3]

4.1.4 Step 4: Why adjusting for L2 fails — L2 is also a mediator

Adjusting for L2 blocks the confounding path (L2 → PDC2 → Y2), but it also blocks part of the causal effect of PDC1 on Y2 that operates through L2. PDC1 affects L2 (worse adherence worsens health), which then affects Y2. By conditioning on L2, we remove this indirect causal effect.

  [saved: fig-dag-step4]

4.1.5 Step 5: The feedback loop that creates the dilemma

The reason L2 is both a confounder and a mediator is the feedback loop: PDC1 → L2 → PDC2. Prior adherence affects future health, which affects future adherence. This is the defining structure of time-dependent confounding — it cannot be solved by any form of standard regression.

  [saved: fig-dag-step5]

4.1.6 Step 6: How L-TMLE solves it

L-TMLE handles the dilemma by intervening on adherence rather than conditioning on it. Instead of asking “what is the outcome for people who happened to have high PDC?” (which selects healthier people), it asks “what would the outcome be if we set everyone’s PDC to high?” The backward iterative targeting step correctly standardises over the distribution of L under the intervention, removing confounding without blocking the causal pathway.

  [saved: fig-dag-step6]

5 Descriptive Statistics

6 Naive Analysis

  [saved: fig-naive]

The naive analysis shows large bias under strong TV confounding, particularly in the Low adherence stratum where the feedback loop (poor PDC -> worse L -> even worse PDC -> higher failure) is most active.

Drug A low 0.5185

7.4 Estimated vs oracle forgiveness curves (fine grid, 10%-wide sliding bins)

This loads pre-computed results from 12_fine_grid_ltmle.R: LTMLE estimates for 10%-wide PDC bins sliding every 5% (e.g., [30-40%], [35-45%], …, [90-100%]). Each bin intervenes by drawing PDC uniformly from that range at every block.

Fine grid loaded: 13 bins x 2 drugs, 26 converged
Coverage in bins >= 50%: 15/18 (83%)

9.4 Quantile-matched drug comparison

The quantile-matching approach maps one drug’s adherence distribution to the other’s at every block, isolating the pharmacological effect from adherence behaviour differences. If Drug A patients had Drug B’s adherence pattern (or vice versa), how much of the drug effect remains?

  [saved: fig-matched-forest]
  [saved: fig-density-quantile]

10 Bias Comparison: Monte Carlo Results

This section loads pre-computed Monte Carlo results from the strong TV confounding scenario. With 4 methods (Naive, Reg(baseline), Reg(baseline+TV), L-TMLE) across 3 strata and 2 drugs, we compare bias performance.

Loaded 4800 MC iteration-rows across 200 iterations
Methods: Naive, Reg (baseline), Reg (baseline + TV), L-TMLE
Strata: Low, Mid, High

10.1 Bias pointrange plot (mean bias +/- SE)

  [saved: fig-mc-bias]

10.2 Drug contrast bias: all methods

  [saved: fig-mc-bias-rd]

10.3 Absolute bias comparison

  [saved: fig-mc-abs-bias]

10.4 Absolute drug contrast bias: all methods

  [saved: fig-mc-abs-bias-rd]

10.5 Regression-only bias comparison

  [saved: fig-mc-bias-reg]

10.6 Drug contrast bias (Risk Difference: Drug A - Drug B)

This figure shows the bias in the 12-month cumulative incidence difference between drugs (RD = Drug A risk - Drug B risk) within each adherence stratum. The oracle RD is computed from the per-drug oracles. If a method is equally biased for both drugs, the RD bias cancels; if it’s differentially biased (e.g., more confounded for one drug), the RD bias persists.

  [saved: fig-mc-bias-reg-rd]

11 Estimand Diagnostic

This section loads the estimand diagnostic results showing how each method’s implicit oracle (what it is actually estimating) compares to the true L-TMLE oracle.

  [saved: fig-estimand-diagnostic]

Key finding: Under strong TV confounding, naive and baseline-regression methods target fundamentally different estimands from L-TMLE, particularly in the Low stratum. The gap is largest where the feedback loop is most active: patients with low PDC experience worsening health (via strong betaP), which further depresses their adherence (via strong alphaL), creating a vicious cycle that confounds the naive estimate.

12 Summary Takeaways

13 Extension: Intercurrent Events

In real-world EHR data, we also face informative censoring, treatment switching, and informative monitoring. These are additional time-varying nodes in the DAG that L-TMLE can handle through the same framework.

  [saved: fig-intercurrent-dag]

13.0.1 How L-TMLE handles intercurrent events

Intercurrent Event Challenge L-TMLE Handling
Informative censoring (LTFU) Patients who drop out are sicker / less adherent IPCW: weight by inverse probability of remaining uncensored
Informative monitoring Sicker patients tested more often — ascertainment bias Include monitoring as an L-node; adjust in the g-model
Treatment switching Patients switch based on failure / side effects Include switching as treatment node or censor at switch
Competing risks (death) Death precludes failure observation Treat as censoring; estimate cause-specific risk

The key point: each intercurrent event becomes an additional node in the DAG. L-TMLE handles them all through the same backward iterative targeting — intervene on treatment/adherence, adjust for confounders, weight for censoring. No new methodology is needed, just more nodes.

14 Mediation Analysis Framework

How much of the treatment effect operates through adherence vs through other pathways? The mediation DAG below shows the decomposition. The total effect of Drug (A) on Outcome (Y) can be decomposed into:

  • Natural indirect effect (NIE): the part mediated through adherence (A → PDC → Y)
  • Natural direct effect (NDE): the part operating through other pathways (pharmacokinetics, resistance barrier, etc.)

In the presence of time-varying confounding, standard mediation (Baron & Kenny, structural equation models) is biased. Longitudinal mediation requires interventional effects or the mediational g-formula.

  [saved: fig-mediation-dag]

14.0.1 What the mediation analysis would estimate

Estimand Question Method
Total effect (TE) What is the overall effect of Drug A vs B on failure? LMTP natural risk comparison
Natural direct effect (NDE) What is the drug effect if we fix adherence at its natural level under Drug B? Interventional direct effect via longitudinal g-formula
Natural indirect effect (NIE) How much of the drug effect operates by changing adherence? TE - NDE, or interventional indirect effect
Controlled direct effect (CDE) What is the drug effect at a specific fixed adherence level? L-TMLE static regime, compare Drug A vs B at same PDC

The quantile-matching analysis in the previous section provides an informal version of the CDE — it fixes the adherence distribution rather than a single level. A formal mediation decomposition (NDE/NIE) requires additional assumptions (cross-world independence) and is a planned extension.

15 Export Figures

All figures rendered during this document are saved to a single .rds file. Load in R with figs <- readRDS("talk_v3_figures.rds") then access individual plots by name (e.g., figs$"fig-oracle").
